sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 16 and Ch. 17 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Some Ch. 17 items reference dated world figures (e.g., Obama, Cameron) exactly as written in the source. Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: Education systems 'throughout the world' vary enormously. A 'major factor' shaping a whole system would operate at what scale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A large, system-wide input such as funding and materials

hints.rationale: System-wide factors operate at a large scale, like funding and materials, rather than individual preferences.

hints.prompt: Student interest, teacher interest, and transportation operate at what scale compared with system-wide funding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A smaller, individual or local scale

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exactly the same as national funding

hints.rationale: Student interest, teacher interest, and transportation are smaller-scale factors than system-wide funding.

hints.prompt: Which two options are individual-level interests rather than a system-wide resource?
hints.rationale: Student interest and teacher interest are individual-level factors, not a system-wide resource.

questions.stem: What are the major factors that affect education systems throughout the world?
questions.explanation: Resources and money are the major factors that shape education systems worldwide, affecting facilities, staffing, and access.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: The word 'informal' (as opposed to formal) education implies learning that happens how?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Through a fixed, written lesson plan

hints.rationale: Informal education happens outside a planned, structured curriculum.

hints.prompt: One option says informal education 'relies on a planned instructional process.' Why is that contradictory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because 'planned instruction' describes FORMAL education, the opposite of informal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because informal education is always carefully planned

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because there is no such thing as formal education

hints.rationale: Planned instruction defines formal education, the opposite of informal.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated, one because it wrongly restricts informal learning to a single location and the other because it actually describes FORMAL education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'only takes place at home' and 'relies on a planned instructional process'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'refers to the learning of cultural norms' and 'describes when students teach their peers'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'describes when students teach their peers' and 'only takes place at home'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'relies on a planned instructional process' and 'refers to the learning of cultural norms'

hints.rationale: Informal learning is not limited to home, and planned instruction is formal education, so both are wrong.

questions.stem: Informal education _________________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: describes when students teach their peers

choices.label: B
choices.text: refers to the learning of cultural norms

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on a planned instructional process

questions.explanation: Informal education refers to learning cultural norms and values outside a formal curriculum, rather than through planned instruction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: A legal case that 'sets a precedent' establishes what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A principle or rule that guides future cases

hints.rationale: A precedent establishes a principle that guides future cases.

hints.prompt: Does 'teacher salary' concern whether a student can attend school?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; teacher salary is about pay, not student access

hints.rationale: Teacher salary concerns compensation, not whether a student may attend school.

hints.prompt: The case concerned students' ability to attend school. Which two options are about funding or pay rather than that?

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'desegregation of schools' and 'average spending on students'

hints.rationale: Average spending and teacher salary are financial matters, not the right to attend school.

questions.stem: The 1972 case Mills v. Board of Education of the District of Columbia set a precedent for __________.
questions.explanation: Mills v. Board of Education (1972) established a precedent for access to education, helping guarantee schooling for students with disabilities.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q5 = A.

hints.prompt: Using education to prepare for and enter a particular CAREER reflects a function of schooling that sorts and prepares people for what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Occupational roles and positions in society

hints.rationale: This function prepares and sorts people into occupational roles.

hints.prompt: 'Socialization' means learning a society's general norms and values. How does channeling someone into a specific CAREER differ from that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It sorts a person into a particular occupational position, beyond general norm-learning

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is exactly the same as socialization

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has nothing to do with education

hints.rationale: Channeling into a career places a person into an occupational position, which goes beyond general socialization.

hints.prompt: Tammie is preparing for a specific career. Which two options describe other functions (creating new knowledge and enforcing conformity) rather than sorting someone into a job?
hints.rationale: Cultural innovation is about generating new knowledge and social control is about enforcing conformity, neither of which is sorting someone into an occupation.

questions.stem: Because she plans on achieving success in marketing, Tammie is taking courses on managing social media. This is an example of ________.
questions.explanation: Using education to prepare for and move into a particular career path reflects social placement, a function of education that sorts people into occupational roles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: A focus on the LABELS people acquire (like 'gifted' or 'struggling') and how those labels shape behavior is a focus on what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Everyday meanings and how they affect individuals

hints.rationale: Focusing on labels and their effects is about everyday meanings affecting individuals.

hints.prompt: Studying labels and their meanings for individuals is analysis at which level?
hints.rationale: Examining labels and meanings for individuals is micro-level analysis.

hints.prompt: Labeling is a MICRO-level focus. Which two options are macro-level theories focused on large structures?
hints.rationale: Conflict and functionalist theories analyze large-scale structures, not micro-level labels.

questions.stem: Which theory of education focuses on the labels acquired through the educational process?
questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism focuses on micro-level processes such as the labels (e.g., 'gifted,' 'struggling') students acquire and how those labels shape outcomes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: Functionalism analyzes how an institution like education contributes to the smooth functioning of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Society as a whole and its parts

hints.rationale: Functionalism examines how institutions serve society and its parts.

hints.prompt: Which of these is a group whose needs education can serve, according to functionalism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Individuals, for example by teaching skills

hints.rationale: Education serves individuals by teaching skills, among other groups.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that education can serve individuals. Before choosing only 'the individual,' what should you check?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether education also serves families and society

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether 'the individual' is the last option

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the answer key prefers short options

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; one true beneficiary is always the whole answer

hints.rationale: If education serves more than one group, a single-group choice may be incomplete, so check the others.

questions.stem: Functionalist theory sees education as serving the needs of _________.
questions.explanation: Functionalists view education as serving the needs of families, society, and individuals simultaneously, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: Separating or ranking students by their measured ability or 'merit' is a process of doing what to them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dividing or ranking them into groups

hints.rationale: The process divides or ranks students into groups.

hints.prompt: The 'hidden curriculum' refers to what, which distinguishes it from ranking students by merit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The unofficial lessons and values students absorb at school

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ranking of students by test scores

hints.rationale: The hidden curriculum is the unofficial values students absorb, not the ranking of students by merit.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe teaching values or culture rather than separating students by measured ability?
hints.rationale: Cultural transmission and the hidden curriculum are about conveying values or culture, not ranking students by ability.

questions.stem: What term describes the separation of students based on merit?
questions.explanation: Sorting is the educational practice of separating or ranking students, ostensibly based on merit or ability.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: Conflict theory analyzes how institutions can favor the already-powerful. A test that is 'culturally biased' would unfairly advantage which group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Students from more privileged, affluent backgrounds

hints.rationale: A culturally biased test unfairly advantages students from privileged backgrounds.

hints.prompt: The conflict-theory critique focuses on CLASS or cultural bias. Which distractor instead raises a purely TECHNICAL scoring issue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'They are scored in a way that is subject to human error'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'They reward affluent students with upper-class questions'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'They do not give children with learning disabilities a fair chance'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'They assume knowledge tied to upper-class culture'

hints.rationale: 'Human error in scoring' is a technical issue, not the class or cultural bias conflict theorists emphasize.

hints.prompt: Conflict theory emphasizes CLASS bias. Which two options raise issues unrelated to social class, namely a scoring-mechanics problem and a multiple-intelligences problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'They are scored in a way that is subject to human error' and 'They do not involve enough test items to cover multiple intelligences'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'They reward affluent students with upper-class questions' and 'They do not give children with learning disabilities a fair chance'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'They do not give children with learning disabilities a fair chance' and 'They are scored subject to human error'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'They do not involve enough items for multiple intelligences' and 'They reward affluent students with upper-class questions'

hints.rationale: Scoring mechanics and multiple-intelligences coverage are not about social class, unlike a class-based critique.

questions.stem: Conflict theorists see IQ tests as being biased. Why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are scored in a way that is subject to human error.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They do not give children with learning disabilities a fair chance to demonstrate their true intelligence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They don't involve enough test items to cover multiple intelligences.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They reward affluent students with questions that assume knowledge associated with upper-class culture.

questions.explanation: Conflict theorists argue that IQ tests are culturally biased, rewarding affluent students whose background matches the upper-class knowledge assumed by the questions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: This item is about a legal guarantee regarding school FUNDING. 'Equitable' spending means spending that is what?
hints.rationale: Equitable spending means spending distributed fairly across students.

hints.prompt: Two options promise that 'all students graduate' or 'all receive an equal education.' Why are these weaker than a funding guarantee?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because schools cannot guarantee every student's graduation or an identical education, but can address funding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because graduation is trivial to guarantee

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because an equal education happens automatically

hints.rationale: Guaranteeing that every student graduates or gets an identical education is not feasible, unlike addressing funding equity.

hints.prompt: Which two options promise outcomes a school cannot actually guarantee for every student?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'all students graduate from high school' and 'all students receive an equal education'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'per-student spending is equitable' and 'the amount spent on each student equals that spent regionally'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the amount spent regionally' and 'all students graduate from high school'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'all students receive an equal education' and 'per-student spending is equitable'

hints.rationale: No school can guarantee that every student graduates or receives an identical education.

questions.stem: Public schools must guarantee that ___________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: all students graduate from high school

choices.label: B
choices.text: all students receive an equal education

choices.label: D
choices.text: the amount spent on each student is equal to that spent regionally

questions.explanation: Per the source, public schools are required to guarantee that per-student spending is equitable, an issue addressed by school-funding court rulings.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q17 = C.

hints.prompt: Advancing a student to the next grade even though they have NOT met the requirements promotes them for what kind of reason?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Social reasons, such as staying with age peers, rather than academic achievement

hints.rationale: It advances students for social reasons rather than academic achievement.

hints.prompt: 'Socialization' means learning society's norms. How does that differ from moving a student up a grade despite unmet requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Socialization is learning norms; the practice here is a grade-advancement policy

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same thing

hints.rationale: Socialization is learning norms, not a policy of advancing students despite unmet requirements.

hints.prompt: Which two options are unrelated to advancing a student to the next grade, one being a policy about discrimination and the other about enforcing conformity?
hints.rationale: Affirmative action addresses discrimination and social control enforces conformity, neither of which is advancing a student a grade.

questions.stem: Allowing a student to move to the next grade regardless of whether or not they have met the requirements for that grade is called ____________.
questions.explanation: Social promotion is advancing a student to the next grade with their peers regardless of whether they have met the academic requirements.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: In sociology, what key quality turns raw 'power' into 'authority'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Being seen as legitimate by those subject to it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using a greater amount of violence

hints.rationale: Authority is power that is regarded as legitimate.

hints.prompt: One option claims 'authority involves intimidation.' Is intimidation the feature that defines authority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; intimidation and coercion are characteristic of raw power, not legitimate authority

hints.rationale: Intimidation reflects coercive power, not the legitimacy that defines authority.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated, one because it attributes intimidation to authority and the other because it wrongly claims authority is always inherited while power is always seized by force?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Authority involves intimidation' and 'Authority is inherited, but power is seized by military force'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Authority is based on the perceived legitimacy of the individual in power' and 'Authority is more subtle than power'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Authority is more subtle than power' and 'Authority involves intimidation'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Authority is inherited, but power is seized by military force' and 'Authority is based on perceived legitimacy'

hints.rationale: Intimidation describes power, and authority is not always inherited nor is power always seized by force, so both are wrong.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses the difference between power and authority?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Authority is more subtle than power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authority is based on the perceived legitimacy of the individual in power.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Authority is inherited, but power is seized by military force.

questions.explanation: Authority is power that is regarded as legitimate by those who are subject to it; legitimacy is the key feature that distinguishes authority from raw power.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: The Senate assigns rank by 'seniority' and long-standing 'custom.' Something based on custom is done because of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has been done that way for a long time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A charismatic leader personally ordered it

choices.label: C
choices.text: A written statute requires it today

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single recent election decided it

hints.rationale: A custom is followed because it has long been done that way.

hints.prompt: Weber's 'legal-rational' authority is based on what, in contrast to custom?
hints.rationale: Legal-rational authority rests on formal written rules, unlike authority based on custom.

hints.prompt: Senate seniority is based on custom. Which two options are the poorest fit, one relying on a leader's personal magnetism and the other on formal written rules?
hints.rationale: Charismatic authority rests on personal magnetism and legal-rational on written rules, neither of which is custom.

questions.stem: In the U.S. Senate, it is customary to assign each senator a seniority ranking based on years of government service and the population of the state he or she represents. A top ranking gives the senator priority for assignments to office space, committee chair positions, and seating on the senate floor. What type of authority does this example best illustrate?
questions.explanation: Authority based on long-standing custom (such as Senate seniority) is traditional authority in Weber's typology.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'constitutional monarch' (a modern king or queen) typically holds what kind of role?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A largely ceremonial role with limited real political power

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total dictatorial control of the state

choices.label: C
choices.text: Command of the military and lawmaking

choices.label: D
choices.text: The power to jail anyone at will

hints.rationale: A constitutional monarch's role is largely ceremonial with limited political power.

hints.prompt: Compared with an elected head of government such as a president or prime minister, a ceremonial monarch has how much real political power?
hints.rationale: A ceremonial monarch generally has less real political power than an elected head of government.

hints.prompt: The question asks who has the LEAST power. Which two figures clearly hold substantial governing power (a national president and an absolute dictator) and so are not the answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'President Barack Obama' and 'North Korean leader Kim Jong-Un'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Queen Elizabeth II' and 'British Prime Minister David Cameron'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'British Prime Minister David Cameron' and 'President Barack Obama'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'North Korean leader Kim Jong-Un' and 'Queen Elizabeth II'

hints.rationale: A national president and an absolute dictator both wield substantial governing power, so neither has the least.

questions.stem: Which current world figure has the least amount of political power?
questions.explanation: As a constitutional monarch, Queen Elizabeth II held a largely ceremonial role with little real political power compared with elected heads of government or a dictator.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (uses dated figures as written in the source). correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'constitutional monarchy' has a monarch whose powers are LIMITED by a constitution. Before those limits existed, such a country likely had a monarch with how much power?
hints.rationale: Before constitutional limits, the monarch would have held nearly unlimited power.

hints.prompt: Why is 'democracy' a poor answer for what constitutional monarchies STARTED as?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because democracies limit rulers' power from the start, whereas constitutional monarchies GAINED such limits over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because democracies are the same as absolute monarchies

hints.rationale: Democracies already limit power, so a monarchy that only later gained limits did not start as a democracy.

hints.prompt: Constitutional monarchies evolved from an earlier MONARCHY. Which two options are not forms of monarchy at all, one being rule by a small elite and the other rule by the people?
hints.rationale: An oligarchy is rule by a few and a democracy is rule by the people, so neither is the monarchy that a constitutional monarchy evolved from.

questions.stem: Many constitutional monarchies started out as:
questions.explanation: Many constitutional monarchies evolved from absolute monarchies as the monarch's powers were gradually limited by constitutions and elected bodies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'dictator' is best described as a ruler who holds what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total, unchecked control over a country

choices.label: C
choices.text: A temporary elected office with checks

hints.rationale: A dictator holds total, unchecked control over a country.

hints.prompt: Leaders such as an elected U.S. president or British prime minister govern under what, unlike a dictator?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elections and constitutional checks on their power

hints.rationale: Elected leaders operate under elections and constitutional checks, unlike a dictator.

hints.prompt: Which two figures led democracies through elections and constitutional checks, and so are not dictators?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Kim Jong-Un' and 'Qaboos bin Said Al Said'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Qaboos bin Said Al Said' and 'David Cameron'

hints.rationale: Cameron and Obama led democratic governments subject to elections and checks, unlike a dictator.

questions.stem: Which of the following present and former government leaders is generally considered a dictator?
questions.explanation: Kim Jong-Un, who rules North Korea with total and unchecked control, is generally considered a dictator.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: In a democracy, ultimate political power rests with whom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The people, for example through voting

hints.rationale: In a democracy, power rests with the people.

hints.prompt: A system in which a king or queen holds the MAJORITY of control describes which form of government, not a democracy?
hints.rationale: A ruler holding the majority of control describes a monarchy, not a democracy.

hints.prompt: This is a 'which is NOT' question. Which two options ARE clearly features of a democracy, and so are not the exception being asked for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'People vote to elect officials' and 'A constitution typically outlines how this government should operate'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A king or queen holds the majority of governmental control' and 'People vote to elect officials'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'One goal is to protect citizens' basic rights' and 'A king or queen holds the majority of control'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'A constitution outlines how this government operates' and 'A king or queen holds the majority of control'

hints.rationale: Voting for officials and having a governing constitution are hallmark democratic features.

questions.stem: Which is not a characteristic of a democracy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A king or queen holds the majority of governmental control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One goal of this type of government is to protect citizens' basic rights.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A constitution typically outlines the foundational ideas of how this government should operate.

questions.explanation: A monarch holding the majority of governmental control describes a monarchy, not a democracy, in which power rests with the people through elected officials.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: Historically, these voting 'tests' were designed not to fairly measure qualifications but to do what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Create an obstacle that kept certain groups from voting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Speed up the counting of ballots

hints.rationale: Such tests were designed as obstacles to keep certain groups from voting.

hints.prompt: Which distractor names a medical or physiological procedure that makes no sense as a voting requirement?
hints.rationale: A blood test is a medical procedure, not a plausible voting requirement.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they name procedures (a medical draw and a polygraph) never used as a voting requirement?
hints.rationale: A blood draw and a polygraph were not used as voting requirements.

questions.stem: In the past, Southern states discouraged African Americans from voting by requiring them to take a _____________________ test.
questions.explanation: Literacy tests were used in the Jim Crow South as a discriminatory barrier to disenfranchise African American voters.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: Sociologists find that voting behavior is shaped by SOCIAL characteristics of voters. Which of the following is a social characteristic of a person?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The physical booth they vote in

choices.label: C
choices.text: The color of the ballot box

hints.rationale: Social class is a social characteristic that shapes voting, unlike physical equipment.

hints.prompt: Race, social class, and ethnicity share what feature that a voting booth does NOT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are social characteristics of the voter

hints.rationale: Race, class, and ethnicity are social characteristics of voters, whereas a voting booth is a physical object.

hints.prompt: This is a 'which does NOT' question. Which two options ARE well-established social influences on voting, and so are not the exception?
hints.rationale: Race and social class are established social influences on voting behavior.

questions.stem: Which factor does not influence voting practices?
questions.explanation: Race, social class, and ethnicity are social factors that influence voting practices; the physical voting booths themselves do not.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: The phrase 'one person, one vote' is a principle about the FAIRNESS of representation. It concerns whether each person's vote carries what?

choices.label: B
choices.text: More weight if the person is wealthy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight based on the voter's age

hints.rationale: The principle is about each person's vote carrying equal weight.

hints.prompt: One option says a voter 'should vote only once a year.' Is a yearly limit what the principle means?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; the principle is about equal weight, not a yearly schedule

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, it sets a yearly voting schedule

hints.rationale: The principle concerns equal weighting of votes, not how often per year one may vote.

hints.prompt: Which two options mistakenly read the phrase as a mechanical rule about how OFTEN one person may cast a ballot?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'One person should not be allowed to vote twice' and 'A voter should vote only once a year'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'All people's votes should count equally' and 'A voter deserves one chance to vote'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A voter deserves one chance to vote' and 'One person should not be allowed to vote twice'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'A voter should vote only once a year' and 'All people's votes should count equally'

hints.rationale: Those two treat the phrase as a mechanical limit on how often a person votes, missing its meaning about equal representation.

questions.stem: Which statement best explains the meaning of "one person, one vote"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One person should not be allowed to vote twice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A voter deserves one chance to vote.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A voter should vote only once a year.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All people's votes should count equally.

questions.explanation: The principle of 'one person, one vote' means each person's vote should carry equal weight in representation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: Conflict theory analyzes society mainly in terms of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Power struggles and inequality between groups

hints.rationale: Conflict theory focuses on power struggles and inequality between groups.

hints.prompt: Karl Marx is most famously associated with which perspective?
hints.rationale: Marx is the classic figure of conflict theory.

hints.prompt: This is a 'which is NOT' question. Which two names are strongly associated WITH conflict-oriented analysis of power, and so are not the exception?
hints.rationale: Marx and Mills are well known for conflict-oriented analyses of power and class.

questions.stem: Which sociologist is not associated with conflict theory?
questions.explanation: George Herbert Mead is a founder of symbolic interactionism, not conflict theory. Mills, Domhoff, and Marx are associated with conflict-oriented analyses of power.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q19 = D.

hints.prompt: What do the Arab Spring, Occupy Wall Street, and the Tea Party most obviously have in common as movements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Groups mobilizing to challenge existing power or policy

hints.rationale: Each is a group mobilizing to challenge existing power or policy.

hints.prompt: Do all three movements share the feature of using VIOLENCE to achieve their goals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; they are not defined by the use of violence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, violence was required for each

hints.rationale: These movements are not defined by the use of violence, so that is not their commonality.

hints.prompt: Which two options wrongly characterize the movements as destructive or violent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'They sought to destroy central government' and 'They used violence as the means of achieving their goals'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'They are examples of conflict theory in action' and 'They can only occur in a representative democracy'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'They can only occur in a representative democracy' and 'They sought to destroy central government'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'They used violence as the means of achieving their goals' and 'They are examples of conflict theory in action'

hints.rationale: Portraying all three as bent on destroying government or using violence is inaccurate.

questions.stem: The Arab Spring, Occupy Wall Street protests, and the Tea Party movement have the following in common:

choices.label: A
choices.text: They sought to destroy central government.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are examples of conflict theory in action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They can only occur in a representative democracy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They used violence as the means of achieving their goals.

questions.explanation: These movements all reflect groups contesting existing power arrangements, which is conflict theory in action.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q21 = B.

hints.prompt: The claim that WEALTH gives some groups outsized power over political systems is fundamentally about what?
hints.rationale: The claim is about inequality in power between groups.

hints.prompt: A perspective emphasizing how the wealthy dominate political systems contrasts most with a perspective that stresses social harmony and shared benefit. That harmony-focused perspective is which?
hints.rationale: Functionalism stresses harmony and shared benefit, unlike a focus on domination by the wealthy.

hints.prompt: Domhoff's argument is about wealth and class power. Which two options are the poorest fit, one focusing on micro-level symbols and the other on social harmony or stability?
hints.rationale: Symbolic interactionism examines micro-level symbols and functionalism stresses harmony, neither of which centers on class-based power.

questions.stem: Sociologist G. William Domhoff's Who Rules America? asserts that wealth is often necessary to exert the most influence over social and political systems. This is a ____ perspective.
questions.explanation: Domhoff's argument that wealth concentrates political influence reflects a conflict theory perspective on power.
